pmos

Data Science Models — Primer

Created using pmos‑learnkit

Primer · Cross-Functional Skills. When a data scientist says "we'll train a model for that," they are choosing from a fairly small menu of well-understood tools — and most product managers nod along without knowing which tool was picked or why it matters. This primer is the menu. It walks the main model families a PM actually meets — regression, classification, the decision-tree workhorses, clustering, recommenders, forecasting, and the natural-language models behind today's AI boom — and for each says what it does, when it's the right call, and what to watch for. It then covers the two things that decide whether a model is any good (how it's judged, and whether you can trust it), and closes on the part that's actually your job: deciding when a model is worth building at all, defining what success means, and working well with the people who build it. Every term of art is defined on first use and every acronym is spelled out — no prior ML (machine learning) background assumed. Source provenance: curation-consensus across the field's teaching canon — Google for Developers, Amazon Web Services, Databricks, and Hugging Face primary docs; Wikipedia for the settled definitions; and practitioner explainers from DataCamp, Towards Data Science, Product School, SAS, and UNSW. 17 verified sources.

What a data science model is

Start with the word "model," because it carries less mystery than it sounds. Google for Developers defines machine learning as "a way to train software, called a model, to make predictions or generate content using data." (Google for Developers) A model is just a piece of software that has learned a pattern from examples and can apply that pattern to something it has not seen before.

The thing that makes a model different from ordinary software is where the logic comes from. In traditional programming, a person writes the rules ("if the email contains these words, mark it spam"). In machine learning, you do the opposite: you show the software thousands of emails already labeled spam or not-spam, and "the model discovers mathematical relationships within" the data, then "applies this learned pattern to make predictions on new information." (Google for Developers) Nobody hand-writes the rule; the model infers it.

Google groups these systems into four families: supervised, unsupervised, reinforcement, and generative. (Google for Developers) Most of this primer lives inside the first two, because that is where the everyday product models sit. The headline takeaway for a PM: a model is a learned pattern, only as good as the examples it learned from — which is why so much of what follows is about data quality and honest measurement, not math.

Further reading: fullstackresearcher.substack.com — Clears up widespread confusion about conjoint analysis: it measures attribute importance only in direct product comparisons, not general preference research.

Further reading: conjointly.com — Research Methods Knowledge Base entry explaining statistical power and its four interrelated components — sample size, effect size, alpha level, and power — and how they trade off in study design.

Supervised vs unsupervised — the master split

The first fork in the road decides everything downstream. Amazon Web Services draws it cleanly: in supervised learning "you train the model with a set of input data and a corresponding set of paired labeled output data" to "predict an output based on known inputs," while in unsupervised learning "you train the model to discover hidden patterns in unlabeled data." (AWS) Put plainly: supervised learning needs an answer key; unsupervised learning does not.

Databricks turns the split into a decision rule a PM can actually use: "Predict outcomes? Supervised learning fits. Explore unknown structure? Unsupervised learning is often the right entry point." (Databricks) The gating question is whether you can get reliable labels — Databricks asks "Do you have reliable labels today? Can you maintain labeling quality as data grows?" Labels are expensive (often humans tagging examples by hand), so this is a real budget question, not a technicality.

Underneath supervised learning sit the two workhorse jobs — regression (predict a number) and classification (predict a category) — and underneath unsupervised sits clustering (find natural groups). The next three sections take them one at a time.

Regression — predicting a number

Regression is the model family for questions whose answer is a quantity. AWS describes it as predicting "a value from a continuous scale" — the classic example being house prices "based on location, age, and number of rooms." (AWS) "Continuous" just means the answer can land anywhere on a range, like $431,200, not one of a few fixed buckets.

Databricks lists the product-relevant uses: regression handles "continuous values, such as demand forecasts, pricing, or risk scores." (Databricks) If your question starts with "how many," "how much," or "what will the value be," you are almost certainly in regression territory — predicted lifetime value, expected delivery time, tomorrow's order volume.

The simplest version, linear regression, fits a straight-line relationship between inputs and the number you want; fancier versions bend that line. For a PM the family matters more than the math: a regression model's output is a number with an error bar, and the right follow-up question is always "how far off is it typically?" — a question the measurement section answers.

Classification — predicting a category

Classification is regression's sibling for questions whose answer is a label, not a number. AWS frames it as predicting "a categorical output," covering "binary classification (two categories)" like spam-or-not and "multiple class classification (more than two categories)." (AWS) Will this customer churn? Is this transaction fraud? Which of five support queues should this ticket go to? All classification.

A subtlety that trips up newcomers: the workhorse algorithm here is named "logistic regression," but it does classification, not regression — AWS notes "logistic regression predicts a categorical output based on one or more inputs." (AWS) Databricks gives everyday examples: "spam versus legitimate email or positive versus negative sentiment." (Databricks)

Most classifiers don't return a hard label — they return a probability ("87% likely to churn"), and someone picks the cutoff above which you act. That cutoff is a product decision, not a math one, because moving it trades one kind of mistake for another. That tradeoff is the whole reason classification models are scored the way they are, which the measurement section unpacks.

Trees and ensembles — the everyday workhorses

For the everyday regression and classification on tables of business data, one family quietly wins most of the time, and it's worth knowing by name. It starts with the decision tree, which Xoriant describes as making "predictions by asking rule-based questions that split data into increasingly homogeneous groups." (Xoriant) Picture a flowchart of yes/no questions ("is the account older than 30 days? did they log in this week?") narrowing down to a prediction. One tree is intuitive but fragile.

The fix is to use many trees — an ensemble (a prediction pooled from many models). The two dominant styles differ in how they combine the trees. Random forest uses "bagging," building trees independently in parallel, each on a random slice of the data, then averaging or majority-voting their answers — GeeksforGeeks notes this is why it stabilizes predictions, and Xoriant adds that "by training many trees on different random subsets of data and averaging them, it stabilizes predictions and prevents overfitting." (GeeksforGeeks) (Xoriant)

The other style, gradient boosting, builds trees "one after another, and each new tree tries to correct the mistakes the previous ones make." (Xoriant) Its famous implementation, XGBoost (short for eXtreme Gradient Boosting), develops "one tree at a time, correcting faults caused by previously trained trees." (GeeksforGeeks) The one-line distinction Xoriant gives: random forest mainly reduces "variance" (instability), while boosting mainly lowers "bias" (systematic error). For a PM, the practical signal is that XGBoost or a random forest is the sensible, unglamorous default for tabular prediction — if a data scientist reaches for one, that's a sign of pragmatism, not a lack of ambition.

Clustering — finding natural groups

Cross into unsupervised territory and the goal changes from "predict the answer" to "find the structure." Clustering is the main tool: AWS says it "groups certain data inputs together" by similarity, with no answer key telling it what the groups should be. (AWS) The model finds the seams in the data; humans interpret what each group means afterward.

The headline product use is segmentation. Databricks describes clustering as "grouping similar data points to reveal structure," enabling customer segmentation and "discovering purchase patterns and product associations without being told which items should be related." (Databricks) Instead of you defining "power users" by a hand-picked rule, clustering surfaces the natural clusters in behavior and lets you name them.

One caution worth carrying: because there is no answer key, there is no single "correct" clustering — change the inputs or the number of groups and the segments shift. Google for Developers notes the patterns clustering finds are ones "humans then interpret," which is both its power and its trap. (Google for Developers) The model proposes; the PM and analyst decide whether the groups are real and useful.

Recommenders — what to show next

The model family behind "you might also like" deserves its own section because so many products live or die by it. Domino describes the dominant approach, collaborative filtering, as recommending "items based on how similar users liked the item" — it works by spotting that people with overlapping tastes tend to want the same things. (Domino) Wikipedia states the underlying assumption: "if persons A and B share similar opinions on one issue, they are more likely to agree on other issues." (Wikipedia)

There are two flavors. User-based filtering finds people like you and recommends what they liked; item-based filtering, as Wikipedia puts it, works as "users who bought x also bought y," building an item-to-item map of what goes with what. (Wikipedia) The contrast is content-based recommendation, which Domino describes as recommending "items with similar metadata tags" — matching a movie's genre and actors rather than other viewers' behavior. (Domino) Real systems usually blend both.

Every PM near a recommender must know its built-in failure: the cold-start problem. Domino frames it as "How do we make recommendations to new users whom we have little to no data about their preferences?" (Domino) Wikipedia adds the mirror case for new items — the system "requires a substantial number of users to rate a new item before that item can be recommended." (Wikipedia) New users and new items are exactly where recommenders are weakest, which is why onboarding flows so often ask you to pick a few interests up front.

Forecasting — predicting over time

Forecasting is regression with a clock attached: the data points come in time order, and yesterday helps predict tomorrow. The canonical model family is ARIMA (Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average), which DataCamp describes as predicting "future values by combining past observations (AR), differencing to achieve stationarity (I), and past errors to refine predictions (MA)." (DataCamp) Unpacking the three letters: AR (autoregressive) uses the series' own recent values as predictors; I (integrated) does "differencing" to strip out trends; and MA (moving average) learns from recent prediction errors.

The one piece of jargon worth holding is stationarity — DataCamp explains it means "the data's mean and variance remain constant," and that ARIMA needs it because the model "cannot effectively account for time-varying variance." (DataCamp) In plain terms, these models assume the world isn't fundamentally shifting underneath the data; when it is (a pandemic, a viral spike), forecasts get unreliable.

ARIMA isn't the only option. DataCamp contrasts it with exponential smoothing, which weights recent observations more heavily: ARIMA gives "stable, long-term forecasts," while exponential smoothing "performs better for short, volatile series." (DataCamp) For a PM, forecasting questions — demand, capacity, churn over the next quarter — should come with a stated horizon and a confidence range; a forecast presented as a single confident number is hiding its own uncertainty.

NLP and large language models

Everything so far worked on numbers and categories; NLP (natural language processing) is the family that works on text, and it is where the last few years got loud. The breakthrough was the transformer architecture. Hugging Face explains its core trick, attention, as letting a model "focus selectively on certain words when processing language," because "a word by itself has a meaning, but that meaning is deeply affected by the context." (Hugging Face) That ability to weigh context is what made fluent machine-generated language possible.

A large language model (LLM) is, in Wikipedia's words, "a neural network trained on a vast amount of text for natural language processing tasks, especially language generation." (Wikipedia) The training is the surprising part. Hugging Face describes it as self-supervised — "the objective is automatically computed from the inputs of the model," so "humans are not needed to label the data" — typically by hiding a word and asking the model to predict it. (Hugging Face) Wikipedia adds that models are "first pretrained to predict the next word" then fine-tuned, often with reinforcement learning from human feedback, abbreviated RLHF. (Wikipedia)

The limitation every PM must internalize is hallucination: Wikipedia warns these models "generate syntactically fluent text that appears factually sound, but is internally inconsistent with training data or factually incorrect." (Wikipedia) Because an LLM is trained to produce plausible-sounding continuations, not true ones, fluency is not evidence of accuracy — a confident wrong answer looks exactly like a confident right one. That single fact should shape any product that puts an LLM in front of users.

How you know a model is good

A model's accuracy number means nothing without knowing how it was measured — and this is where PMs get misled most often. The foundation is the train/test split. As one walkthrough puts it, the test set is "kept hidden during training" and "used to measure how well the model generalizes to unseen data." (Monica Ashok) Scoring a model on data it trained on is like grading students on the exact questions they studied — meaningless.

The failure it guards against is overfitting — when a model "memorizes rather than generalizes," learning the training data's noise instead of the real pattern. (Monica Ashok) An overfit model looks brilliant in the lab and falls apart in production. So whenever you see a glowing accuracy figure, the first question is "on held-out data the model never saw?"

For classifiers, the scoreboard is the confusion matrix and the two numbers it yields. Towards Data Science defines precision as how often a positive call is correct ("correct 98% of the time") and recall as the share of real positives caught ("identified 94% of all Malignant Tumors"). (Towards Data Science) They trade off: "if we push recall to 1.0 then the precision drops." The F1 score "balances both." (Towards Data Science) Which to favor is a product call — a fraud filter that misses fraud (low recall) fails differently from one that blocks good customers (low precision), and only the PM can say which mistake is worse.

Bias, fairness, and the black box

A model can score well and still be untrustworthy, which is why two more ideas belong in every PM's vocabulary. The first is the difference between interpretability and explainability. SAS defines interpretability as "determining how an analytical model or algorithm came to its conclusions" — visible inputs and process, which simple models like decision trees and linear regression offer. (SAS) Explainability instead addresses "why an algorithm produces those responses" even when the internals stay obscure — the case for complex "black box" models you cannot read directly. (SAS)

The second is bias, and the stakes are concrete. UNSW BusinessThink documents Amazon's hiring tool, which — "trained on predominantly male resumes" — discriminated against women, and after attempted fixes "couldn't be sure the AI wouldn't find other ways to discriminate." (UNSW BusinessThink) A model learns whatever patterns are in its training data, including the unfair ones, and it cannot tell a legitimate pattern from a discriminatory one.

The genuinely contested part — worth flagging because it's easy to over-trust — is that explanation tools are not a clean safety net. UNSW warns it is "possible to intentionally hide the discriminatory behaviour of a predictor and make the black-box model appear neutral through interpretation tools like partial dependence plots." (UNSW BusinessThink) The honest posture: interpretability buys real trust, explanation-after-the-fact buys less than it appears to, and in high-stakes domains a PM should treat "the model says so" as the start of a question, not the end of one.

Further reading: uxdesign.cc — Analysis of Google's "messy middle" consumer decision-making model for product managers, explaining how customers oscillate between exploration and evaluation before purchase and how products can influence that journey.

Further reading: highagency.com — Essay arguing high agency — the ability to take initiative and find solutions in difficult situations — is the defining trait of exceptional people, with practical heuristics for spotting high-agency individuals.

What this means for product managers

The first PM judgment is whether to use a model at all. Product School's checklist says reach for ML when a problem "handles very complex logic," "scales-up fast," needs real-time adaptation or personalization, and "has existing examples of actual answers" — and avoid it when simple rules suffice, when the problem "requires full interpretability," or "requires 100% accuracy." (Product School) A model is the wrong tool for anything that must be perfect or fully auditable.

The second judgment is defining success honestly, and this is where Google for Developers issues the sharpest warning in this whole primer. It insists on tracking two metric types — business metrics like "revenue, click-through rate, or number of users" and model metrics like precision or RMSE (root mean squared error, a regression error measure) — and then says plainly: "Don't confuse model success with business success," because "Great model metrics don't necessarily imply improved business metrics." (Google for Developers) Its remedy is to "deploy the model to 1% of users and then monitor the business metric." A model that aces its lab score but moves no real-world number is a failure, however impressive the score.

The third judgment is collaboration. Product School's guidance is that "PMs who come prepared with a clear, preferably data-driven, problem and hypothesis will have a much more productive discussion," and that the PM owns the tradeoffs — "interpretability vs. accuracy" and "false positives vs. false negatives." (Product School) The recurring theme: the math belongs to the data scientist, but the problem definition, the success metric, and the choice of which mistake is acceptable belong to you.

Pitfalls and how to work with data scientists

The most common pitfall is reaching for a model too early. A PM's guide on Towards Data Science urges validating "Is ML actually needed?" first, since "in many cases a heuristics-based approach can work better" until simple rules stop scaling. (Towards Data Science) A few if-statements that ship this week often beat a model that ships next quarter — and you can always upgrade once the rules visibly strain.

The second pitfall is underestimating the data bill. The same guide notes model choice depends on data availability (supervised needs labels), on latency (real-time "stream" versus scheduled "batch" processing), and on having enough clean data — it suggests a rough floor of "at least 10k examples" and a user-feedback loop, "because we ultimately live or die by business performance." (Towards Data Science) Garbage in, garbage out is not a slogan here; it is the single biggest determinant of whether a model works.

The third is treating the model as done at launch. Product School cautions that "ML creates tech debt" and that scientists "are not Software Engineers" — models drift as the world changes and need monitoring and retraining. (Product School) Pair that with Google's advice to roll out to 1% and watch the business metric (Google for Developers), and the collaboration pattern is clear: bring a sharp problem, agree the two metrics up front, decide which error you can live with, and plan for the model to be maintained, not just shipped.

Further reading: evanmiller.org — Interactive calculator for determining A/B test sample sizes, parameterized by statistical power and significance level.

Where this connects — adjacent topics

This primer is the map of model families; several neighbors go deeper on the pieces it only points at:

  • Data engineering — the pipelines that feed models; supervised learning's "reliable labels as data grows" problem is a data-engineering problem before it's a modeling one.
  • Product analytics & metrics — where the business metrics that judge a model are defined and instrumented; the "don't confuse model success with business success" warning lives here.
  • A/B testing & experimentation — the discipline behind "deploy to 1% and monitor"; how you actually prove a model moved a real number.
  • Generative AI & LLM product patterns — the application layer on top of the language-model section: retrieval, prompting, evaluation, and designing around hallucination.
  • Data governance, privacy & AI ethics — the formal side of the bias and black-box section: fairness auditing, consent, and accountability for automated decisions.
  • SQL & exploratory data analysis — the hands-on skills for inspecting the data a model learns from before anyone trains anything.

References

  1. developers.google.com ·cited· (T1)

    Google for Developers defines machine learning as "a way to train software, called a model, to make predictions or generate content using data" — a fundamentally different paradigm from hand-coded rules, where the model discovers mathematical relationships in data and applies them to new inputs. It names four system types: supervised, unsupervised, reinforcement, and generative.

  2. aws.amazon.com ·cited· (T1)

    AWS frames the master split: supervised learning trains "with a set of input data and a corresponding set of paired labeled output data" to "predict an output based on known inputs," while unsupervised learning trains "to discover hidden patterns in unlabeled data." Regression predicts "a value from a continuous scale," classification predicts a categorical output, and clustering "groups certain data inputs together."

  3. databricks.com ·cited· (T1)

    Databricks gives the practical choice rule: "Predict outcomes? Supervised learning fits. Explore unknown structure? Unsupervised learning is often the right entry point" — gated on "Do you have reliable labels today?" It maps the uses: regression for "continuous values, such as demand forecasts, pricing, or risk scores," classification for "assigning input data to predefined classes," and clustering for "grouping similar data points to reveal structure" like customer segmentation.

  4. huggingface.co ·cited· (T1)

    Hugging Face explains transformers: an attention layer lets a model "focus selectively on certain words when processing language," because "a word by itself has a meaning, but that meaning is deeply affected by the context." LLMs are trained with self-supervised learning — "the objective is automatically computed from the inputs of the model" so "humans are not needed to label the data" — via next-word or masked-word prediction, then fine-tuned.

  5. developers.google.com ·cited· (T1)

    Google's ML project guide insists on two metric types up front: business metrics ("revenue, click-through rate, or number of users") and model metrics ("Root Mean Squared Error, precision, or recall"). Its warning is the core PM lesson: "Don't confuse model success with business success" — "Great model metrics don't necessarily imply improved business metrics" — so "deploy the model to 1% of users and then monitor the business metric."

  6. xoriant.com ·cited· (T2)

    Xoriant explains the tree family: a decision tree "makes predictions by asking rule-based questions that split data into increasingly homogeneous groups." Random forest trains "many trees on different random subsets of data and averaging them" to stabilize predictions and prevent overfitting. Gradient boosting builds "many small decision trees one after another, and each new tree tries to correct the mistakes the previous ones make"; XGBoost adds regularization. Random forest reduces variance; boosting lowers bias.

  7. geeksforgeeks.org ·cited· (T2)

    GeeksforGeeks contrasts the two ensemble styles: random forest uses "bagging (Bootstrap Aggregating)" to build trees independently in parallel, each on a bootstrap sample, then averages or majority-votes. XGBoost "develops one tree at a time, correcting faults caused by previously trained trees" sequentially via gradient descent, with "built-in regularization (L1 and L2)."

  8. domino.ai ·cited· (T2)

    Domino frames recommenders: collaborative filtering recommends "items based on how similar users liked the item," working through user or item similarity, while content-based recommendation matches "items with similar metadata tags." It names the cold-start problem: "How do we make recommendations to new users whom we have little to no data about their preferences?"

  9. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    Wikipedia defines collaborative filtering as "a method of making automatic predictions (filtering) about a user's interests by utilizing preferences or taste information collected from many users," on the principle that people who agree on one issue tend to agree on others. User-based finds similar people; item-based works as "users who bought x also bought y." Cold start arises from "insufficient data on these new entries."

  10. datacamp.com ·cited· (T2)

    DataCamp defines an ARIMA model as combining "past observations (AR), differencing to achieve stationarity (I), and past errors to refine predictions (MA)" for time-series forecasting. Stationarity (constant mean and variance) is required. ARIMA gives "stable, long-term forecasts," while exponential smoothing weights recent observations more and "performs better for short, volatile series."

  11. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    Wikipedia defines a large language model as "a neural network trained on a vast amount of text for natural language processing tasks, especially language generation," pretrained "to predict the next word" then fine-tuned (e.g. via RLHF). It flags hallucination: models "generate syntactically fluent text that appears factually sound, but is internally inconsistent with training data or factually incorrect."

  12. towardsdatascience.com ·cited· (T2)

    Towards Data Science breaks down classifier scoring via the confusion matrix (true/false positives and negatives). Precision is how often a positive prediction is correct ("correct 98% of the time"); recall is the share of actual positives caught ("identified 94% of all Malignant Tumors"); F1 "balances both." The precision-recall tradeoff: "if we push recall to 1.0 then the precision drops."

  13. medium.com ·cited· (T2)

    This walkthrough defines overfitting as when a model "memorizes rather than generalizes," learning the training data's noise instead of broad patterns. The train/test split keeps the test set "hidden during training" and "used to measure how well the model generalizes to unseen data" — it "protects against overfitting" and "mimics real business scenarios."

  14. blogs.sas.com ·cited· (T2)

    SAS distinguishes interpretability — "determining how an analytical model or algorithm came to its conclusions," the inputs and processes (decision trees, linear regression are more interpretable) — from explainability, which addresses "why an algorithm produces those responses" even when the internals stay obscure. Opaque "black boxes" make decisions users can't follow.

  15. businessthink.unsw.edu.au ·cited· (T2)

    UNSW BusinessThink documents real-world bias: Amazon's hiring tool, "trained on predominantly male resumes," discriminated against women, and after correction they "couldn't be sure the AI wouldn't find other ways to discriminate." It warns that black-box models "are often opaque," and that explanation tools like partial dependence plots can be manipulated to "make the black-box model appear neutral" while staying discriminatory.

  16. productschool.com ·cited· (T2)

    Product School lists when to use ML (complex logic, scales fast, real-time adaptation, personalization, existing answer examples) and when to avoid it (simple rules suffice, no adaptation, "requires full interpretability," "requires 100% accuracy"). On collaboration: "PMs who come prepared with a clear, preferably data-driven, problem and hypothesis will have a much more productive discussion," must make tradeoffs (accuracy vs interpretability, false positives vs false negatives), and remember "ML creates tech debt."

  17. towardsdatascience.com ·cited· (T2)

    This PM guide stresses validating "Is ML actually needed?" since "in many cases a heuristics-based approach can work better" until rules stop scaling. Model choice turns on data availability (supervised needs labels), latency (stream vs batch), and complexity; data should have "at least 10k examples" and a user-feedback loop, because "we ultimately live or die by business performance."

  18. fullstackresearcher.substack.com ·cited· (T2)

    Clears up widespread confusion about conjoint analysis: it measures attribute importance only in direct product comparisons, not general preference research. Explains when to use it versus broader discrete-choice or stated-preference methods.

  19. uxdesign.cc ·cited· (T3)

    Analysis of Google's "messy middle" consumer decision-making model for product managers, explaining how customers oscillate between exploration and evaluation before purchase and how products can influence that journey.

  20. evanmiller.org ·cited· (T3)

    Interactive calculator for determining A/B test sample sizes, parameterized by statistical power and significance level.

  21. conjointly.com ·cited· (T3)

    Research Methods Knowledge Base entry explaining statistical power and its four interrelated components — sample size, effect size, alpha level, and power — and how they trade off in study design.

  22. highagency.com ·cited· (T3)

    Essay arguing high agency — the ability to take initiative and find solutions in difficult situations — is the defining trait of exceptional people, with practical heuristics for spotting high-agency individuals.

pmos Source: docs/pmos/primer/2026-06-14_data-science-models.html · Created using pmos‑learnkit
